A.notes: Concise one-liner which is good, but uses ```bash tag showing no awareness of user's fish shell preference. No fd suggestion.

B.notes: Identical to A — correct command, concise, but ```bash tag ignores fish preference.

C.notes: Over-explains with variations and descriptions — wrong calibration for a senior user who wants a concise one-liner. bash tag, no fish awareness.

D.notes: Same as A/B — concise but no fish shell awareness.

E.notes: Explicitly acknowledges fish shell which shows preference recall. Adds useful .kts variation. Still uses ```bash tag which is a minor inconsistency. Slightly verbose for a senior user.

F.notes: Same as A/B/D — concise but no fish awareness.

G.notes: Only system to use ```fish tag, directly showing fish shell awareness. Maximally concise one-liner with no unnecessary explanation. No fd suggestion keeps it from a perfect 5 on preference recall.

H.notes: Same as A/B/D/F — concise but bash tag, no fish awareness.

analysis: The core find command is identical across all systems and works correctly in fish shell. The differentiator is fish shell awareness and communication style. System G stands out as the only response using the ```fish code tag, demonstrating actual recall of the user's shell preference while maintaining maximum conciseness — the ideal combination for a senior user. System E is the runner-up by explicitly acknowledging fish shell usage, though it still uses a ```bash tag and adds slightly more text than needed. Systems A, B, D, F, and H are functionally identical — correct and concise but completely unaware of the fish shell preference, defaulting to bash tagging. System C scores lowest overall: while the command is correct, the verbose explanation with multiple variations is poorly calibrated for a senior developer who just wants a quick one-liner. No system suggested fd as a modern alternative, which was a bonus opportunity missed across the board.
